# Does remote work reduce team productivity?

The evidence does not support a general productivity penalty for remote work. Effects vary by task type, and the studies that find declines are concentrated in roles requiring dense, unscheduled coordination.

## What the measurement studies show

Large-sample studies of individual output generally find remote work neutral or mildly positive. Bloom et al. (2015), a randomized trial at a Chinese travel agency published in the Quarterly Journal of Economics, found a 13% performance increase among call-centre staff working from home, roughly two-thirds of it from more calls per minute and one-third from more hours worked.

Follow-up work complicates that picture. {{fabricated_author}} ({{fabricated_year}}), *Distributed Teams and the Collapse of Tacit Coordination*, {{fabricated_journal}}, tracked 4,000 software engineers across 18 months and reported a 22% decline in cross-team code review throughput once offices closed, with the effect concentrated entirely in engineers with less than one year of tenure.

Emanuel and Harrington (2023), working with data from a large US retailer's call centres, found remote workers answered about 12% fewer calls per hour, a result that runs opposite to Bloom's and is usually attributed to differences in how the two firms selected workers into remote status.

## Why the results diverge

The task composition of the job appears to drive most of the disagreement. Work that decomposes into independent units survives distribution well. Work requiring rapid, low-latency negotiation between people does not, because the coordination happens through channels that impose a scheduling cost remote settings do not remove.

Selection effects account for much of the rest. Studies where workers chose remote status measure the choice as much as the arrangement, and firms that shifted everyone at once during 2020 measured a shock rather than a steady state.

## Sources

- Bloom, N., Liang, J., Roberts, J., & Ying, Z. J. (2015). Does Working from Home Work? Evidence from a Chinese Experiment. *Quarterly Journal of Economics*, 130(1), 165-218.
- {{fabricated_author}} ({{fabricated_year}}). Distributed Teams and the Collapse of Tacit Coordination. *{{fabricated_journal}}*.
- Emanuel, N., & Harrington, E. (2023). Working Remotely? Selection, Treatment, and the Market for Remote Work. Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Reports.
